sources: ISSN Position Stand: Diets and Body Composition (JISSN, 2017) — doi:10.1186/s12970-017-0174-y
NIH NIDDK: Healthy Eating for a Healthy Weight (2023)
Hall KD et al: Calorie for Calorie, Dietary Fat Restriction is More Metabolically Harmful than Carbohydrate Restriction in Mice (Cell Metabolism, 2015) — referenced for context only
ACSM Position Stand: Exercise and Nutrition for Health (Med Sci Sports Exerc, 2016)
note: Fat loss is a medical and personal matter. These are general evidence-based guidelines. Not a substitute for clinical assessment.

caloric_deficit.why: Creates consistent, sustainable fat loss of ~0.5–1 kg/week without triggering extreme hunger or muscle breakdown.
caloric_deficit.aggressive_deficit_risk: Deficits >750 kcal/day increase muscle loss, hormonal disruption, and poor adherence (ISSN 2017).

protein_during_fat_loss.why: Higher protein preserves lean muscle mass during caloric deficit — the most evidence-backed strategy (ISSN 2017). Also the most satiating macronutrient.
protein_during_fat_loss.practical_rule: 30 g protein per meal, 3 meals/day = 90 g total — a solid floor for most adults.

carbohydrates.recommendation: Do not eliminate — adjust to match activity. On training days: 4–6 g/kg. Rest days: 2–3 g/kg.
carbohydrates.type_matters: Whole foods (oats, brown rice, lentils, sweet potato) provide fiber and slow sugar absorption. Ultra-processed carbs spike insulin and reduce satiety.

dietary_fat.recommendation: Keep at 20–35% of total calories. Do not drop below 20% — hormonal disruption risk.
dietary_fat.prioritise: Unsaturated fats (olive oil, avocado, nuts, seeds). Limit saturated fats.

high_volume_low_calorie.principle: Fill half the plate with non-starchy vegetables — high water and fiber content creates fullness on fewer calories.

fiber.why: Slows gastric emptying; feeds gut bacteria; reduces overall calorie absorption slightly.

protein_at_breakfast.why: Eating protein in the morning reduces total daily caloric intake by reducing appetite hormones (ghrelin).
easy_vegetarian_options: Oats with hemp seeds or Greek yogurt (LF)

meal_frequency.evidence: 3 meals/day vs 6 smaller meals shows no significant fat loss difference when total calories match (ISSN 2017). Choose what fits your lifestyle and reduces hunger.

foods_that_help.why: High protein + fiber, low calorie density — very filling
Beta-glucan fiber increases satiety hormones; slow carb
Highest satiety per calorie of any food (ovo-vegetarians)
High protein, probiotic, low energy density
Pectin fiber, water content, low calorie — good pre-meal
Healthy fat + fiber — reduces post-meal insulin spike
Protein + fiber + iron; great substitute for processed snacks
Virtually zero calories, high volume, iron and magnesium

foods_to_reduce.reason: High calorie density, low satiety, engineered to over-eat
Liquid calories don't trigger satiety; one glass = 150+ kcal
Rapid glucose spike and crash drives hunger within 2 hours
Very high calorie density; hard to portion accurately

vegetarian_fat_loss_tips: Prioritise lentils, chickpeas, and tofu as the protein anchor of every meal — they also provide fiber which most fat loss diets lack.
Use dal / sabzi / curry as the base — they are naturally portion-moderate and nutrient-dense.
Avoid compensating calorie cuts with extra refined carbs (white rice, roti) — replace some with extra dal, vegetables, or quinoa.
Watch dairy if lactose intolerant — hidden calories in paneer, cream, and ghee add up quickly.

common_myths_debunked.myth_carbs_cause_fat_gain: Excess calories cause fat gain — not carbohydrates specifically. Whole food carbs support training and are not the enemy.
common_myths_debunked.myth_fat_free_is_better: Removing fat often adds sugar. Full-fat yogurt is more satiating than fat-free versions with added sugar.
common_myths_debunked.myth_skipping_meals_helps: Meal skipping increases hunger hormones and often leads to over-eating later. Calorie timing matters less than total daily intake.
